EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A disturbance in the northern branch of the jet stream was able to phase with low pressure in the southern branch of the jet stream over the Southeast Coast on the night of the 18th. These two systems combined to develop a strong area of low pressure that slowly track up the Mid-Atlantic Coast during the 19th. The low was able to tap into moisture from the Gulf of Mexico and the Atlantic Ocean causing copious amounts of precipitation to develop. High pressure to the north kept plenty of cold air in place causing the precipitation to fall in the form of snow.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A storm total of 16.0 inches of snow was reported at The Mall.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper-level disturbance combined with plenty of moisture and an unstable atmosphere to trigger showers and thunderstorms during early morning hours of the 9th. With locally heavy rain in a short period of time, this lead to an isolated area of flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Canal Road at Reservoir Road was closed due to flash flooding.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A potent cold front combined with plenty of instability to trigger showers and thunderstorms. Some thunderstorms were severe, producing damaging winds and large hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Quarter inch hail was reported near American University.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A potent cold front combined with plenty of instability to trigger showers and thunderstorms. Some thunderstorms were severe, producing damaging winds and large hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees were down on Reservoir Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A potent cold front combined with plenty of instability to trigger showers and thunderstorms. Some thunderstorms were severe, producing damaging winds and large hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Large sections of two trees were blown down over the National Mall.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front combined with an unstable atmosphere to trigger showers and thunderstorms during the afternoon and evening hours of the third. An isolated thunderstorm did become severe, producing damaging winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Six trees were down in the northeastern portion of the District.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A potent area of low pressure developed off the Southeast Coast on Sunday, March 1st. This system slowly moved up the Mid-Atlantic coast Sunday night tapping into plenty of moisture from the Gulf of Mexico as well as the Atlantic Ocean. With cold air in place, precipitation fell in the form of snow Sunday night into Monday, March 2nd. Snow tapered off early Monday afternoon as the storm system headed up towards New England.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall totaled up to 8.0 inches at the National Arboretum. A report of 7.8 inches was received from Logan Circle, and 6.0 inches of snow fell at the Dalecarlia Reservior.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An area of low pressure tracked through the Tennessee Valley into the Ohio Valley on the 27th spreading precipitation across the District of Columbia. Precipitation began as snow, but a change over to sleet, and eventually freezing rain occurred on the afternoon and evening of the 27th. Freezing rain finally changed to rain on the afternoon of the 28th before ending.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Ice accumulations around one quarter of an inch were estimated in Washington D.C. Snow and sleet accumulation up to an inch was also reported in Washington D.C.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A wave of low pressure riding along a stalled boundary triggered rain and thunderstorms. Heavy amounts of rain in a short period of time lead to isolated areas of flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Beach Drive at Blagden Avenue was closed due to flash flooding.
